coredata.dc:title: Facile single step synthesis of carbon nano-sponges as a fluorimetric sensor for 4-nitroaniline and pseudocapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Anusuyadevi, K.
Balamurugan, R.
Chandra Bose, A.
Velmathi, S.
coredata.dc:description: Generally, synthesis of most of the doped carbon nanomaterials except carbon dots required multiple steps and stringent conditions. Herein we have attempted a single step synthesis of nitrogen and oxygen doped carbon nano-sponges (NS) by simple heating of precursors in dimethylsulfoxide rather than conventional methodologies. The structure, morphology and optical properties of NS was well investigated by various characterization techniques. Deleterious effect of 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) has seized our attention as it could be a potential threat to living beings and in addition optical sensors for 4-NA is sparse. Therefore, we have examined the fluorescent sensing capability of NS in determination of 4-NA with limit of detection 0.88 ppm. The practical applicability of NS was accomplished in soil samples. High surface area, porous nature and functionalities over NS have encouraged us to enquire its ability as supercapacitor. NS demonstrated pseudocapacitive behavior with appreciable specific capacity of 262 C/g at 1 A/g and evinced perceptible cyclic stability and reversibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterial synthesis
Doped nanocarbon
4-nitroaniline detection
Sphere of quenching
Energy storage

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Anusuyadevi Kathiresan acknowledges DST-INSPIRE Fellowship (IF-170902) for funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of NS by FE-SEM under different magnifications of (a) 2 μm (b) 100 nm (c) Average size distribution of nano-sponges. HR-TEM images of NS at (d) 200 nm (e) 50 nm magnifications. (f) SAED pattern of NS.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of NS (b) Deconvoluted spectrum of C1s (c) Deconvoluted spectrum of N1s (d) Deconvoluted spectrum of O1s.
(a) Excitation and emission spectra of NS (13 μg/mL) in ethanol (b) Excitation dependent emission of NS (13 μg/mL) at varying excitation wavelength from 307 nm to 387 nm in ethanol.
Emission of NS in ethanol. (13 μg/mL) with increasing concentration of 4-NA (0–85 μM) (Excitation: 377 nm & emission: 502 nm).
Modified Stern-Volmer plot (Perrin's quenching sphere of action) of NS in ethanol (13 μg/mL) with increasing concentration of 4-NA from 0 to 85 μM.
Estimation of increasing percentage of 4-NA in spiked soil samples.
(a) CV curves of NS at different scan rates 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150 and 200 mV/s (b) Plot between peak current and square root of scan rate.
(a) GCD curves of NS at different current density 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 A/g (b) Specific capacity with increasing current density 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 A/g.
Cyclic stability and Coulombic efficiency of NS at current density of 20 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Facile single step synthesis of carbon nano-sponges as a fluorimetric sensor for 4-nitroaniline and pseudocapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic and Polymer Synthesis Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirappalli, 620 015, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Organic and polymer synthesis Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirappalli- 620 015 India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials Laboratory, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirapalli, 620 015, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanomaterials Laboratory, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirapalli-620015 India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Generally, synthesis of most of the doped carbon nanomaterials except carbon dots required multiple steps and stringent conditions. Herein we have attempted a single step synthesis of nitrogen and oxygen doped carbon nano-sponges (NS) by simple heating of precursors in dimethylsulfoxide rather than conventional methodologies. The structure, morphology and optical properties of NS was well investigated by various characterization techniques. Deleterious effect of 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) has seized our attention as it could be a potential threat to living beings and in addition optical sensors for 4-NA is sparse. Therefore, we have examined the fluorescent sensing capability of NS in determination of 4-NA with limit of detection 0.88 ppm. The practical applicability of NS was accomplished in soil samples. High surface area, porous nature and functionalities over NS have encouraged us to enquire its ability as supercapacitor. NS demonstrated pseudocapacitive behavior with appreciable specific capacity of 262 C/g at 1 A/g and evinced perceptible cyclic stability and reversibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials have been an impressive area of research to material science community for past few decades due to its exceptional optical, electrical and mechanical properties [1,2]. Astonishing optical and electrical properties of carbon nanomaterials are widely exploited in fluorescent sensing and energy storage devices respectively. The efficacious of those properties can be enhanced by suitable doping and functionalisation. It is conspicuous from the literature that presence of heteroatoms could improve both fluorescence as well as energy storing capability [3–5]. Accordingly, we have chosen precursors rich in heteroatoms (Triphenylamine aldehyde & 2-amino-3-hydroxy pyridine) instead of opting for multistep synthesis.

Noteworthy properties of carbon nanomaterials can also be tuned by adopting different synthesis methodologies. Generally, nanomaterials are synthesized by top-down and bottom-up approach. Supremacy of bottom-up approach over top-down relies in its efficiency to furnish regularly shaped nanosystems [6,7]. The commonly employed techniques in bottom-up approach includes hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave etc [8–10]. Nevertheless, carbonization requires sophisticated reaction equipment, necessitates higher temperature of approximately 600–800 °C, multistep synthesis and in addition decomposition of chemical compounds would expel toxic gases. Hence it is necessary to develop an alternative facile method for effortless preparation of nanocarbon. Thus, we have adopted a peculiar synthesis strategy of heating precursors in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) at 120 °C. Herein we have attempted to carbonize precursors in DMSO as it remains an unexplored solvent in synthesis of nanocarbon. Hence, we have investigated the ability of DMSO in carbonization.

Tremendous surge in number of industries for the production of essential commodities could upgrade country's economy but the effluents released from industries possibly pollute soil to a greater extent and turned it unfit for the survival of living organisms [11]. Among various effluents, 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) has grabbed our interest due to its carcinogenic nature and its ill effects like methemoglobinemia, diarrhoea, damages liver and respiratory system due to its wide deployment in synthesis of pesticides, pharmaceuticals etc [12]. As far as our knowledge, reported carbon nanomaterials for detection of 4-NA [13,14] (listed in Table S1) are sparse and in addition, synthesis of those materials required sophisticated equipments. Thus, we have established fluorimetric sensor by relatively simple synthesis for discrimination of 4-nitroaniline.

Though there are surplus of renewable sources of energy are available, lack of storage materials leads to energy crisis. Supercapacitors are extraordinary storage system as it has the potentiality to have long life with appreciable energy and power density than regular capacitors [15]. On understanding the necessity of energy storage devices researchers have developed diverse of metallic nanomaterials in fabricating supercapacitors [16]. However, the less toxic nature of carbon nanomaterials has motivated researchers to examine its potency as supercapacitor. Due to mesoporous nature and high surface area, graphene and carbon nanotubes were widely explored as energy storage devices [17,18]. Here we have attempted to study the electrochemical properties of comparably least explored doped nanocarbon. It is observed from Table S2 that most of the synthesis process of doped systems required stringent conditions to adopt desired nanostructures such as multiple step synthesis, higher carbonization temperature (∼600–800 °C) and annealing for activation [19–24]. We have acquired simplest methodology to prepare NS which was capable to produce higher capacitance than the reported methods. The high surface area and functionalities over the surface of nanocarbon are responsible for significant specific capacity.

Here in this work, we have adopted an unusual and simple single step bottom-up approach for synthesis of nano-sponges (NS) instead of traditional bottom-up technique. NS was well characterized by microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. NS in ethanol exhibited maximum emission at 502 nm when excited at 377 nm. NS acted as an inner filter effect (IFE) platform, static (sphere of quenching) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) for the specific determination of 4-NA. Estimation of 4-NA in soil sample was also speculated. In addition, NS was also acted as a pseudocapacitor with significant capacitance of 262 C/g with appreciable reversibility and cyclic stability.

Generally, nanomaterials are synthesized by conventional synthesis methodologies like hydrothermal, solvothermal, refluxing, microwave etc. Herein we have adopted different strategy to synthesize nanocarbon by simple heating of precursors in 1 mL of DMSO. 1:1 M ratio of triphenylamine aldehyde (synthesized by reported literature [25]) (A) and 2-amino-3-hydroxy pyridine (B) was dissolved in 1 mL of DMSO and heated at 120 °C for 16 h. The resultant mixture was poured in crushed ice after it attained room temperature. The separated brown colored solid was filtered and stored at room temperature.

The synthesis conditions were optimized by varying ratio, temperature and time. The emission of product obtained varied with different ratios of A & B such as 1:0.5, 0.5:1 and 1:1. The maximum emission was achieved at ratio of 1:1. Similarly temperature and time of reaction mixture in DMSO was varied as T = 80 & 120 °C and t = 4, 8, 16 & 24 h. The maximum emission was achieved on heating 1:1 ratio of A and B in 1 mL DMSO and heated at 120 °C for 16 h (Fig. S1).

It was observed from Table S3 that solvents utilized for the synthesis of reported carbon nanomaterials are only polar solvents like water, formamide, acetone and alcoholic solvents. We have inferred from the table that polar solvents could facilely assist the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials. Thus, initially synthesis of the material was attempted in solvents like methanol, ethanol and acetonitrile (polarity index of methanol- 5.1, ethanol- 5.2, acetonitrile- 5.8) but no carbonization was observed. We couldn't perform synthesis in aqueous medium due to poor solubility of precursors.

Thus, we have tried the synthesis with polar aprotic solvent DMSO (as it possesses high polarity index of 7.2) which resulted in carbonization of precursors. The formation of carbon nano-sponges were initiated by formation of imine bond between triphenylamine aldehyde and 2-amino-3-hydroxy pyridine. High temperature and prolonged reaction time of 120 °C for 16 h assisted nucleation and growth of nano-sponges.

The successful formation of carbon nano-sponges in DMSO could be accounted by two factors such as polarity index and viscosity. From Table S3, it was perceived that polar solvents are extensively explored for the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials. Hence, high polarity index of DMSO could be one of the responsible factors. Though ethanol, methanol and acetonitrile are also polar, no carbonization was observed wherein the role of viscosity comes into play. Due to the high viscosity of DMSO, it slowed down the mobility of precursors in solution that could plausibly assist the nucleation and growth of nano-sponges unlike other solvents. Thus, the plausible reason for the carbonization of precursors in DMSO could be attributed to relatively high polarity index and viscosity of DMSO.

The structure and morphology of NS was studied by various microscopic and spectroscopic studies like X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM), High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) and Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). XRD of NS exhibited broad peak at 2θ = 20° with interlayer spacing (d) of 0.44 nm (Fig. 1 .). The broad diffraction pattern and higher d value than graphitic carbon conspicuously depicted poor crystalline nature of nano-sponges [26,27]. This was because of the presence of hetero-functionalities over surface of NS [28]. The amorphous nature of NS was further corroborated by diffused rings of Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2 (f)).

The morphology of NS was investigated by microscopic studies. FE-SEM images at different magnifications are shown (Fig. 2(a) &b). The images at lower magnifications depicted bunch of buds like morphology when it was zoomed into higher magnifications nano-sponges like morphology was visualized (Fig. 2(a) and b). Wide range of sponge size distribution was observed while the average size of such nano-sponges was 149.2 nm (Fig. 2(c)). Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) was performed to identify the composition of NS.

It was observed that atomic percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are 76.55%, 13.83% and 9.63% respectively which substantiated the carbonization of precursors (Fig. S2). The morphology of NS was further studied by HR-TEM. The images of HR-TEM at different magnifications are given in (Fig. 2(d) and (e). Nano-sponges morphology was authenticated by HRTEM.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption studies at 77 K was carried out to investigate surface area and porosity of NS. NS exhibited hysteresis curve corresponded to type-IV isotherm that demonstrated mesoporous nature of the material. Hysteresis loop at 0–0.2 P/P0 denoted micropores on NS. Pores over the surface of sponges contributed to micropores. While small hysteresis loop at 0.3–0.9 P/P0 ascribed to voids between nano-sponges attributed to the mesoporous characteristic of NS (Fig. S3a). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of NS was 309.96 m2/g. Pore size distribution was calculated by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) plot evidenced the mesoporous nature of NS with average pore radius and pore volume are 1.54 nm 0.890 cm3/g respectively (Fig. S3 b).

The functional groups of NS were further substantiated by FT-IR spectroscopy. The broad peak at 3412 cm−1 attributed to hydrogen bonded OH stretching, peak at 3049 & 1587 cm−1 accounted for aromatic C–H & CC respectively. The absence of carbonyl peak of aldehyde & sharp primary amine stretching peaks (two bands of symmetric & asymmetric stretching) and the presence of CN stretching peak at 1487 cm−1 denoted the carbonization takes place through the formation of CN [29]. Correspondingly, heterocyclic CN (pyridine), C–N and C–O stretching vibrations at frequencies 1323, 1277 & 1177 cm−1 independently [30] (Fig. S4). Thus, NS is composed of aromatic core decorated with oxygen and nitrogen rich moieties. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy furnished three distinct peaks corresponded to binding energies at 285.25 eV, 400.14 eV and 532.75 eV accorded with C1s, N1s and O1s respectively (Fig. 3 (a)). The atomic percentage of C1s, N1s and O1s were 81.05%, 10.08% and 7.90% respectively. The survey spectrum was deconvoluted in order to understand the nature of elements present. The intensity of resolved peaks are in analogous with atomic percentage of elements. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum manifested peaks at 284.4 ± 0.004 eV corresponded to CC. Both C–N and C–O resonated around same region at 285.2 ± 0.18 eV. CO and CN appeared at 286.1 ± 0.1 eV [31,32] (Fig. 3(b)). The corresponding deconvoluted spectrum of N1s elaborated information about nature of nitrogen. Peaks at 398.6 ± 0.09 eV, 399.6 ± 0.03 eV and 400.1 ± 0.02 eV conceded the existence of pyridinic nitrogen [33], C–N and CN [34] correspondingly (Fig. 3(c)). The deconvoluted O1s yielded peaks at 531 ± 0 eV, 532.3 ± 0.03 eV and 535.5 ± 0 eV. The FWHM of 3 eV oxidised some of the C–O to CO which is included in O1s deconvoluted spectrum. The CO peak at 531 ± 0 eV was only due to the oxidation of C–O under unstable conditions.

Peak at 532.3 ± 0.03 eV confirmed presence of hydroxy (C–OH) group in nano-sponges and 535.5 ± 0 eV corresponded to moisture [35] (Fig. 3(d)). Thus, the type of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in NS was decoded by deconvolution of survey spectrum.

Photophysical properties of NS was recorded by dissolving solid NS in ethanol by sonication for 90 min. The UV–vis spectrum of NS in ethanol exhibited a peak at 243 nm due to π-π∗ transition of aromatic units and a peak around 300 nm was accorded to n-π∗ transition (Fig. S5). The excitation and emission spectra in ethanol were given in Fig. 4 (a). The maximum intensity of excitation spectrum was observed at 377 nm. The maximum emission of NS appeared at 502 nm when excited at 377 nm with Stokes shift greater than 120 nm. Emission of NS was due to diverse surface states includes functionalities over NS, dangling bonds and sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons which acted as a trap site of excitation energy [36]. The effect of excitation wavelength on emission of NS was studied (Fig. 4(b)). Emission maximum of NS was red-shifted from 484 to 502 nm with increasing intensity while altering λ ex from 307 nm to 427 nm. Excitation dependent identity of NS was accredited to diverse surface defects. The radiative recombination energy of excitons confined in those varied surface states were altered with different excitation energy. This excitation dependent phenomenon substantiated the origin of emission from surface state [37]. Fluorescence decay studies of NS with excitation and emission wavelength 377 & 502 nm respectively was elucidated in Fig. S6. It manifested an average lifetime of 0.19 ns with biexponential decay (T1 = 1.66 × 10−10 s & T2 = 2.4 × 10−9 S). The rapid decay was due to non-radiative recombination and biexponential decay demonstrated that emission of NS from uneven surface states [38].

Fluorescence of NS was investigated under solvents with different polarities to examine the influential role of solvents on emission of NS (Fig. S7). It was perceived that λ max of NS was shifted to lower energy with diminishing intensity on varying solvents of diverse polarity. The gradual bathochromic shift on moving from dioxane (461 nm) to ethanol (502 nm) was ascribed to positive solvofluorochromism of NS [39]. The maximum intensity of NS was observed in dioxane while the emission of NS was decreased on rising the polarity of the solvent and completely quenched in water.

As we have aspired to detect an environmentally toxic target (4-NA), we planned to utilize green solvent as our sensing medium. Unfortunately, NS remains non-emissive in aqueous medium hence we have chosen ethanol as solvent in subsequent sensing experiments as it is also a green solvent. The quantum yield (ɸ) of NS in ethanol was 17.1% with respect to Rhodamine-6G.

Generally, the emission of nanomaterials is highly susceptible for perturbations under various conditions like pH, high ionic concentration, and prolonged photo-irradiation. Hence the effect of such factors on fluorescence of NS was scrutinized. The emission of NS was inspected on altering pH from 2 to 10. No significant variation in emission of NS was perceived (Fig. S8(a)). Effect of sodium chloride on the fluorescence of NS was probed by gradually rising the concentration of sodium chloride from 0.1 M to 0.6 M. No recognizable alteration of emission was noticed (Fig. S8(b)). Photo-irradiation of NS was perused for 15 min. There was no sharp disparity in emission was observed. Thus, fluorescence of nano-sponges was stable on photo-irradiation. Therefore, as prepared NS shown substantial stability under agitating circumstances (Fig. S8(c)).

Carbon nano-sponges were synthesized by simple heating in DMSO instead of conventional synthesis protocols. High emission of NS was achieved under optimized conditions. The structure, morphology, surface functional groups and photophysical properties of sponges were analyzed in detail. Then we have employed NS for the fluorimetric determination of 4-nitroaniline.

The fluorimetric response of NS for selective discrimination of 4-nitroaniline was enquired. 13 μg/mL of NS was prepared in ethanol and employed in sensing studies. NS in ethanol exhibited maximum emission at 502 nm when excited at 377 nm. 86% of emission intensity of NS was quenched on addition of 100 μM of 4-NA. It was conspicuous from stability studies that emission of NS remained intact even with 15 min of photoirradiation. However, the emission of NS was declined instantly on addition of 4-NA. Thus, sensing studies of NS was carried out without incubation period (Fig. S9).

The emission of NS was studied by gradually rising the concentration of 4-NA. As the concentration of 4-NA was increased there was a linear decrease in emission of NS was beheld. The decreasing emission was saturated when the concentration of 4-NA was 85 μM (Fig. 5 ). The selective response of NS was probed by analyzing emission of NS in presence of other amino & nitro derivatives such as 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene, dinitrobenzene, nitrobenzene, 3-nitrobenzoic acid, 2-aminophenol, 3-aminophenol, 2-nitroaniline, 3-nitroaniline, 2-nitrophenol, 3-nitrophenol, 4-nitrophenol, 4-nitrotoluene, 3-aminobenzoic acid, 2,4-dinitrophenyl hydrazine, aniline and 2,4-dinitroaniline (4-NA and all interferents solutions were also prepared in ethanol) (Fig. S10(a)). The expressive quenching of NS was achieved only in presence of 4-NA whereas no complete quenching in emission was noticed with other interferents. The specific determination of 4-NA by NS was explored by examining the emission of NS in existence of both 4-NA and interferents. The emission at 502 nm was suppressed significantly irrespective of other interferents (Fig. S10(b)). Thus, NS could discriminate 4-NA with reliable sensitivity and selectivity without incubation period. The specialty of NS is that it could specifically discriminate 4-NA even in presence of its corresponding ortho and meta isomers. The selective and sensitive detection of 4-NA by NS was due to the synergistic effect of IFE, PET and sphere of quenching which will be explained in section 4.2.

Linearity of Stern-Volmer (S–V) plot was destructed at higher concentrations which witnessed the involvement of multiple mechanisms in quenching [40]. (Fig. S11). S–V plot of NS was linear at lower concentration of 4-NA ranges from 0 to 30 μM while the plot ascended upwards above 30 μM. The lowest detection limit (LOD) calculated from the linear plot (Fig. S11 inset) as 0.88 ppm.

Intending to determine the type of quenching, lifetime measurements were investigated. It was noted that the lifetime of NS of 0.19 ns was altered on addition of 4-NA (Fig. S5). The lifetime of NS after the addition of 4-NA (100 μM) was 0.12 ns with two exponential decay T1 = 1.037 × 10−10 and T2 = 2.38 × 10−9 s (Fig. S12). Change in lifetime confirmed the role of dynamic quenching. Thus, it could be either due to electron transfer or energy transfer. However, there was no overlapping region of absorption spectrum of 4-NA with emission spectrum of NS. This excluded the possibility of Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). Hence the mechanism responsible for quenching is PET. The electron rich NS transfer its electron to electron-deficit 4-NA.

As presence of only dynamic quenching would furnish a linear S–V plot, non-linear S–V plot could be accounted for both dynamic quenching and “Perrin's quenching sphere of action”. Quenching sphere of action is also a type of static quenching wherein no distinct ground state complex is formed instead the emission of fluorophore is quenched due to the vicinity of quencher and fluorophore in a confined sphere. Equation of Perrin model is given below [41] (Fig. 6 )
F 0
/ F = A
e
K p
[ Q ]
+ B
wherein F0/F is the emission of NS before and after addition of quencher 4-NA. [Q] is the concentration of 4-NA, Kp is Perrin quenching constant, A & B are constants. The equation was solved by adopting method reported by Sergio et al. [41] whom assumed value of A and B as 1 and 0 respectively. Perrin quenching constant was calculated from slope of plot between ln F0/F Vs [Q]. The value of Kp is calculated from plot between ln F0/F Vs [Q] as 2.7 × 104 M-1 from Fig. 6. Perrin quenching constant is related to radius of quenching sphere as follows:
K p = V q
N = 4 / 3
π
N
R 3

Where Vq represents volume of the sphere at which fluorophore (NS) and quencher (4-NA) are proximity to each other, N is Avogadro constant and R is the radius of sphere at which quenching occurs. The volume of radius of the quenching sphere was calculated as 22.13 Å.

Interestingly, we have noticed a distinct spectral overlap between excitation of NS and absorption of 4-NA. Hence quenching was also attributed to the absorption of excitation energy of NS by 4-NA. Thus, IFE was also responsible for quenching of NS in presence of 4-nitroaniline. The overlap between excitation of NS and absorption of 4-NA denoted primary IFE (Fig. S13). The synergistic effect of all above elaborated mechanisms are responsible for selective and sensitive detection of 4-NA.

In general, essence of the developed sensors could be realized only when it is capable of discriminating target in real samples. Soil can be possibly contaminated due to improper disposal of 4-NA from industries. Hence, we have collected field soil which was ground well and it was spiked with different percentage of 4-NA varying from 0.0005% to 0.01% (Fig. 7 ). The decrement in emission of NS was evident with arousing percentage of 4-NA treated soil samples.

Accessible pore radius and surface area furnish enhanced electrochemical activity by facile charge transfer. Hence, we are intrigued to investigate electrochemical performance of NS using three electrode system and 2 M KOH as electrolyte. NS as a working electrode, saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode and platinum foil as counter electrode. Capacitive activity of NS was studied by Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and Galvanostatic Charge Discharge (GCD) measurements. Electrical Double Layer Capacitance (EDLC) and pseudocapacitance are the two conventional energy storage mechanisms in supercapacitors [42]. Pseudocapacitive is common among metallic nanostructures while EDLC is prevailing mechanism in undoped nanocarbon [43,44]. CV curves of NS are shown in Fig. 8 (a) under various scan rates 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 200 mV/s. Distinct oxidation and reduction peaks of NS with various scan rates demonstrated pseudocapacitive behaviour of NS. Redox reaction was due to the reversible reaction of functionalities over the surface of NS with electrolyte [23]. Small shift towards higher potential in CV curves on increasing scan rate was associated with developed resistance because of restricted movement of electrolyte ions in approaching NS. The rise in peak current of redox peaks on increasing scan rate was due to mobility of ions in electrode-electrolyte interface. Linearity plot between peak current and square root of scan rate determined the charge storage is dominated by diffusion-controlled reactions Fig. 8(b).

GCD property of NS was studied within potential of 0–0.39 V with different current densities (1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 A/g) and the plot was given in Fig. 9 (a). The plateau in voltage substantiated the pseudocapacitive behaviour and the specific capacity at different current densities 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 A/g are 262, 250, 241, 230, 211, 192 and 175 C/g respectively. There was a pronounced decrease in specific capacity with rising current density. This was due to the accumulation of ions in diffusion pathway increased the resistance of NS. Even at high current density of about 20 A/g, 67% of specific capacity of the material was retained (Fig. 9(b)). Thus, the robust nature NS was dictated. The symmetric nature of GCD depicted the reversible redox nature of NS.

The cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency were given in Fig. 10 . The cyclic stability was investigated at fixed current density of 20 A/g. 89% of its initial specific capacity was retained even after 20,000 cycles. The reversible nature of NS remained unaffected after numerous cycles. The efficiency of NS as a supercapacitor manifested that it could be fabricated as a perfect energy storage system.

Electrochemical Impedance of NS was investigated within a range of frequencies between 100 kHz and 100 mHz in 2 M KOH. Nyquist plot of NS was fitted with equivalent circuit using EC-lab software (version 11.36). Z-fit method was shown in Fig. S14. The calculated series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) are 0.811 Ohm and 8.27 Ohm respectively. The lower series resistance denoted high conductivity of NS while the high charge transfer resistance depicted resistance of electrons from electrolyte to NS. The values of Rs and Rct demonstrated high storage ability of NS.

Atypical method of carbonization was adopted for synthesis of NS rather than traditional techniques. Appreciable surface area, stability and emission of NS was utilized in development of fluorimetric sensor for toxicant 4-NA and as an energy storage device. Emission of NS was specifically arrested by 4-NA by sphere of static quenching, PET and IFE with LOD 0.88 ppm. Presence of toxicant was also examined in spiked soil samples. Electrochemical results portrayed pseudocapacitance behavior of NS with specific capacity of 262 C/g at 1 A/g current density. The cyclic stability and reversibility of NS described its efficiency as energy storage system. NS obtained by adopting simple synthesis strategy could act both as a remarkable optical sensor and an impeccable supercapacitor.
Kathiresan Anusuyadevi - Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Methodology; Roles/Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing.
Ramalingam Balamurugan- Data curation; Formal analysis; Methodology; Writing – review & editing.
Arumugam Chandra Bose- Formal analysis; Investigation; Project administration; Resources; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing – review & editing.
Sivan Velmathi- Formal analysis; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Project administration; Resources; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Anusuyadevi Kathiresan acknowledges DST-INSPIRE Fellowship (IF-170902) for funding.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101659.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanomaterials for electronics, optoelectronics, photovoltaics, and sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Jariwala, V.K. Sangwan, L.J. Lauhon, T.J. Marks, M.C. Hersam, Carbon nanomaterials for electronics, optoelectronics, photovoltaics, and sensing. Chem. Soc. Rev, 42 (2013) 2824-2860.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The synthesis, properties and uses of carbon materials with helical morphology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Shaikjee, N.J. Coville, The synthesis, properties and uses of carbon materials with helical morphology. J. Adv. Res, 3 (2012) 195-223.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring the field emission property of nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes by controlling the graphitic/pyridinic substitution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ghosh, M. Kumar, T. Maruyama, Y. Ando, Tailoring the field emission property of nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes by controlling the graphitic/pyridinic substitution. Carbon, 48 (2010) 191-200.
